query: clinical features and diagnostic approach for chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia

retrieved_docs.title: Kearns-Sayre Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Chronic Progressive External Ophthalmoplegia
retrieved_docs.content: Mitochondrial myopathy characterized by drooping of the eyelids (ptosis) and paralysis of the extraocular muscles (ophthalmoplegia).

retrieved_docs.title: Kearns-Sayre Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: CPEO must be differentiated from other disorders associated with ophthalmoplegia. These include: Isolated chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia (CPEO) Oculopharyngeal muscular dystrophy Myotonic dystrophy Isolated oculopharyngeal myopathy Mendelian chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia (CPEO) associated with multiple deletions of mtDNA such as POLG

retrieved_docs.title: Ptosis Correction -- Introduction -- Chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia (CPEO)
retrieved_docs.content: CPEO is a mitochondrial myopathy causing bilateral symmetrical involvement of the extraocular muscles. Manifestations begin in childhood or adolescent age and progress slowly during adulthood. Bilateral symmetrical involvement is the first symptom followed by bilateral ophthalmoplegia. Due to the symmetric involvement of extraocular muscles, patients often do not complain of diplopia. As the muscles of facial expression are involved, patients develop an expressionless face (Hutchinson's face) Diagnosis is confirmed by muscle biopsy which shows ragged red fibers due to enlarged mitochondria.

retrieved_docs.title: Kearns-Sayre Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Kearns-Sayre syndrome (KSS) is a clinical subtype of chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia (CPEO).

retrieved_docs.title: Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the epidemiology of internuclear ophthalmoplegia. Review the eye exam of a patient with internuclear ophthalmoplegia. Outline brainstem signs that may assist in localizing the site of the medial longitudinal fasciculus lesion in a patient with internuclear ophthalmoplegia. Summarize interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to enhance outcomes for patients affected by internuclear ophthalmoplegia. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The main differential diagnoses include: VI nerve palsy Lateral gaze palsy One-and-half syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The physical examination in most patients with internuclear ophthalmoplegia helps practitioners to arrive at a diagnosis. However, investigations like neuroimaging assist in finding the underlying cause. MRI can be a valuable tool in this regard, and up to 75% of patients may have a visible lesion. For demyelinating lesions, proton density imaging is considered to be ideal. Supplementary blood and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) studies may be undertaken to detect uncommon causes. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: A simple physical examination is often all that is required to diagnose a case of internuclear ophthalmoplegia. Although most cases of isolated internuclear ophthalmoplegia have a favorable prognosis, patients should be evaluated to determine the underlying cause. The medial longitudinal fasciculus with its periventricular location represents an accessible site for stem cell delivery to test newer innovative neuroprotective and restorative therapies such as stem cell remyelination.

retrieved_docs.title: Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The hallmark of internuclear ophthalmoplegia is impaired adduction in the eye ipsilateral to the affected medial longitudinal fasciculus, which can range from mild limitation to severe restriction of adduction. There is nystagmus in the abducting eye associated with this, which usually lasts for a few beats. [7] Some patients with internuclear ophthalmoplegia may converge to a near target. The dissociation of the medial rectus function during horizontal saccades and convergence helps to confirm a medial longitudinal fasciculus lesion, ruling out other causes of medial rectus weakness. In all cases of internuclear ophthalmoplegia, the physician should be on the lookout for other brainstem signs to confirm the diagnosis of internuclear ophthalmoplegia and localize the site of the medial longitudinal fasciculus lesion. Lesions of the medial longitudinal fasciculus associated with adjacent brainstem structures can produce a variety of ocular motor signs and are called internuclear ophthalmoplegia plus syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: Melas Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Kearns-Sayre is a rare neuromuscular disorder that may also present with visual disturbances, short stature, hearing loss, and ataxia. The characteristic visual findings of Kearns-Sayre, including chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia, atypical retinitis pigmentosa, and pigmentary degeneration of the retina, distinguish it from MELAS. Furthermore, patients with Kearns-Sayre syndrome often exhibit more frequent cardiac manifestations in addition to the visual symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Findings of internuclear ophthalmoplegia on a physical exam need a central nervous system workup to determine the cause. an interprofessional team including nurses should be involved in the workup. The treatment depends on the cause.

retrieved_docs.title: Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia is an ocular movement disorder caused by a lesion of the medial longitudinal fasciculus. It is characterized by impaired adduction of the ipsilateral eye with nystagmus of the abducting eye. This activity describes the pathophysiology and presentation of internuclear ophthalmoplegia and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in its diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment depends on the underlying cause. Acute strokes require hospitalization and neurological evaluation. Other pathologies require management by a physician (e.g., MS, infections, SLE). Most patients with demyelination, infectious, and traumatic etiologies show complete recovery. Patients with cerebrovascular disorders had a less favorable recovery. Recovery is said to be more likely if internuclear ophthalmoplegia is isolated than if other neurological signs accompany it. According to some studies, recovery is also said to be less likely if there was a visible lesion causing internuclear ophthalmoplegia.

retrieved_docs.title: Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis depends on the etiological agent causing the ophthalmoplegia. Brainstem demyelination and trauma do not have a good prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Although there is impaired adduction in the ipsilateral eye, some patients may have normal convergence. This is called dissociation of convergence and is an important sign which helps distinguish internuclear ophthalmoplegia from pseudo internuclear ophthalmoplegia (e.g., myasthenia gravis, third nerve palsy). It was initially thought that patients with medial longitudinal fasciculus lesion below the level of the oculomotor nucleus (posterior internuclear ophthalmoplegia of Cogan) had retained convergence and lesions above the oculomotor nucleus level (anterior internuclear ophthalmoplegia of Cogan) had absent convergence. However, recent studies have disproved this theory, and retained convergence is thought to reflect the innate ability to converge to near targets. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2169
retrieved_docs.content: Acute disorder of hemodialysis, sudden acquired retinal degeneration, and متلازمة اختلال غسيل الكلى (dialysis dysfunction syndrome) are all described as having a clinical course characterized by sudden onset. The “sudden onset” qualifier indicates that the manifestations of these conditions develop rapidly, which is a key feature for clinicians to recognize when diagnosing and managing these disorders. Understanding that a rapid, abrupt presentation is common to these entities helps guide timely evaluation and appropriate therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2239
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all conditions in which the nerve serves as the primary site of pathological findings. This highlights that the nerve is a common locus for diverse neuropathic processes, ranging from acute hemorrhagic injury to inflammatory, toxic, and genetic sensory neuropathies. Recognizing the nerve as the affected structure helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and tailor management strategies for patients presenting with these nerve‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2361
retrieved_docs.content: Headache; including migraine is classified as a condition that belongs to the broader category of Nervous system and sense organ diseases. The category Nervous system and sense organ diseases also includes the child entity اضطراب العين (eye disorder). Recognizing that both headache—including migraine—and eye disorders are subtypes of Nervous system and sense organ diseases helps clinicians organize related symptoms, consider shared pathophysiological mechanisms, and guide appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2159
retrieved_docs.content: Migraine and Headache Syndromes are categorized as a subcategory of the broader condition known as اعتلال عصبي عضلي (neuromuscular disease). This hierarchical relationship suggests that migraine and headache disorders may be viewed within the context of neuromuscular pathology, highlighting the importance of considering underlying neuromuscular factors when diagnosing and managing patients presenting with these headache syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2602
retrieved_docs.content: Acute disorder of hemodialysis, First use syndrome of dialysis, Sudden acquired retinal degeneration, and متلازمة اختلال غسيل الكلى (dialysis dysfunction syndrome) are medical conditions that share a common clinical characteristic: a sudden‑onset course. In each of these disorders the disease manifests abruptly, indicating that their clinical course is defined by a rapid appearance of symptoms. Recognizing this sudden‑onset pattern is important for timely diagnosis and appropriate management of complications related to hemodialysis, initial dialysis use, retinal degeneration, or dialysis dysfunction.

Peripheral involvement includes peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis and peripheral focal retinitis with retinochoroiditis. Across all entities, the choroidea (choroid) is the anatomical locus of the characteristic lesions, underscoring its central role in pathophysiology, clinical assessment, precise diagnosis, and tailored management of choroidal and retinal disorders.
